(**US 2**) is a component of the [United States Numbered Highway System](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highway_System "United States Numbered Highway System") that connects [Everett, Washington](/wiki/Everett,_Washington "Everett, Washington"), to the [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") (UP) of the US state of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), with a separate segment that runs from [Rouses Point, New York](/wiki/Rouses_Point,_New_York "Rouses Point, New York"), to [Houlton, Maine](/wiki/Houlton,_Maine "Houlton, Maine"). In Michigan, the highway runs through the UP in two segments as a part of the [state trunkline highway system](/wiki/Michigan_Highway_System "Michigan Highway System"), entering the state at [Ironwood](/wiki/Ironwood,_Michigan "Ironwood, Michigan") and ending at [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace,_Michigan "St. Ignace, Michigan"); in between, US 2 briefly traverses the state of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"). As one of the major transportation arteries in the UP, US 2 is a major conduit for traffic through the state and neighboring northern [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest") states. Two sections of the roadway are included as part of the [Great Lakes Circle Tours](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Circle_Tours "Great Lakes Circle Tours"), and other segments are listed as state-designated [Pure Michigan Byways](/wiki/Pure_Michigan_Byway "Pure Michigan Byway"). There are several memorial highway designations and historic bridges along US 2 that date to the 1910s and 1920s. The highway runs through rural sections of the UP, passing through two national and two state forests in the process.

**US Highway 2** (**US 2**) is a component of the [United States Numbered Highway System](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highway_System "United States Numbered Highway System") that connects [Everett, Washington](/wiki/Everett,_Washington "Everett, Washington"), to the [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") (UP) of the US state of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), with a separate segment that runs from [Rouses Point, New York](/wiki/Rouses_Point,_New_York "Rouses Point, New York"), to [Houlton, Maine](/wiki/Houlton,_Maine "Houlton, Maine"). In Michigan, the highway runs through the UP in two segments as a part of the [state trunkline highway system](/wiki/Michigan_Highway_System "Michigan Highway System"), entering the state at [Ironwood](/wiki/Ironwood,_Michigan "Ironwood, Michigan") and ending at [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace,_Michigan "St. Ignace, Michigan"); in between, US 2 briefly traverses the state of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"). As one of the major transportation arteries in the UP, US 2 is a major conduit for traffic through the state and neighboring northern [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest") states. Two sections of the roadway are included as part of the [Great Lakes Circle Tours](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Circle_Tours "Great Lakes Circle Tours"), and other segments are listed as state-designated [Pure Michigan Byways](/wiki/Pure_Michigan_Byway "Pure Michigan Byway"). There are several memorial highway designations and historic bridges along US 2 that date to the 1910s and 1920s. The highway runs through rural sections of the UP, passing through two national and two state forests in the process.

The route of what became US 2 was used as part of two Indian trails before European settlers came to the UP, and as part of the Michigan segments of the [Theodore Roosevelt International Highway](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_International_Highway "Theodore Roosevelt International Highway") and the King's International Highway [auto trails](/wiki/Auto_trail "Auto trail") in the early 20th century. The state later included these trails as part of [M‑12](/wiki/M-12_(Michigan_highway) "M-12 (Michigan highway)") when the first state highway trunklines were designated in 1919. Most of M‑12 was redesignated as part of US 2 when the US Highway System was created on November 11, 1926. Since the 1930s, several changes have reshaped the highway's routing through the UP. One such alteration eventually created a [business loop](/wiki/Business_loop "Business loop") that connected across the state line with [Hurley, Wisconsin](/wiki/Hurley,_Wisconsin "Hurley, Wisconsin"), and others pushed an originally inland routing of US 2 closer to the [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") shoreline. With the creation of the [Interstate Highway System](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System"), part of US 2 was rerouted to coincide with the new [Interstate 75](/wiki/Interstate_75_in_Michigan "Interstate 75 in Michigan") (I‑75), though in the 1980s, the U.S. Highway was truncated and removed from the I‑75 freeway, resulting in today's basic form.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

According to a 2006 regional planning committee report, US 2 is a key highway for Michigan, providing its main western gateway. The roadway plays "an important role in the transportation of goods across the northern tier of states in the Midwest",[[3]](#cite_note-WUPPRDR-4) and is listed on the [National Highway System](/wiki/National_Highway_System_(United_States) "National Highway System (United States)") (NHS) for its entire length.[[4]](#cite_note-NHS-MI-5) The NHS is a network of roadways important to the country's economy, defense, and mobility.[[5]](#cite_note-NHS-6) Together with [M‑28](/wiki/M-28_(Michigan_highway) "M-28 (Michigan highway)"), US 2 is part of a pair of primary trunklines that bridge the eastern and western sides of the UP.[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7) The 305.151 miles (491.093 km) of roadway in Michigan is divided into a 109.177-mile (175.703 km) western segment and a 195.974-mile (315.390 km) eastern segment,[[1]](#cite_note-PRFA-1) interrupted by a section that runs for 14.460 miles (23.271 km) in the state of Wisconsin.[[7]](#cite_note-WisDOT-8)

### Western segment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Western segment")]

US 2 enters Michigan from Wisconsin for the first time north of downtown [Hurley, Wisconsin](/wiki/Hurley,_Wisconsin "Hurley, Wisconsin"), and [Ironwood, Michigan](/wiki/Ironwood,_Michigan "Ironwood, Michigan"), over the state line that runs along the [Montreal River](/wiki/Montreal_River_(Wisconsin%E2%80%93Michigan) "Montreal River (Wisconsin–Michigan)"). The highway crosses the river into [Gogebic County](/wiki/Gogebic_County,_Michigan "Gogebic County, Michigan") and passes a [welcome center](/wiki/Welcome_center "Welcome center") on the way into a commercial district north of downtown. Running along Cloverland Drive, US 2 meets its only [business route](#Business_route) in Michigan at Douglas Boulevard.[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9) The business route was previously a full loop that ran west through downtown Ironwood and crossed the border into Hurley and back to the main highway. The [Wisconsin Department of Transportation](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Transportation "Wisconsin Department of Transportation") has removed the signage on their side of the border, which reduced the loop to a business spur that ends at the state line.[[9]](#cite_note-WisDOT01-02-10)[[10]](#cite_note-WisDOT03-04-11) US 2 continues eastward through UP woodlands to the city of [Bessemer](/wiki/Bessemer,_Michigan "Bessemer, Michigan"). While bypassing the community of [Ramsay](/wiki/Ramsay,_Michigan "Ramsay, Michigan"), the highway crosses a branch of the [Black River](/wiki/Black_River_(Gogebic_County) "Black River (Gogebic County)"). The roadway enters [Wakefield](/wiki/Wakefield,_Michigan "Wakefield, Michigan") on the south side of Sunday Lake, meeting M‑28 at a stoplight in town. As the US Highway leaves Wakefield, it turns southeasterly through the [Ottawa National Forest](/wiki/Ottawa_National_Forest "Ottawa National Forest"),[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12) crossing Jackson Creek and two branches of the [Presque Isle River](/wiki/Presque_Isle_River "Presque Isle River"). US 2 and [M‑64](/wiki/M-64_(Michigan_highway) "M-64 (Michigan highway)") merge and [run concurrently](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") over the second branch of the Presque Isle in the community of [Marenisco](/wiki/Marenisco,_Michigan "Marenisco, Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12) This concurrency has the lowest traffic volume along the entire length of the highway within the state; in 2010 the [Michigan Department of Transportation](/wiki/Michigan_Department_of_Transportation "Michigan Department of Transportation") (MDOT) recorded a daily average usage along the stretch of 770 vehicles, compared to the overall average of 5,188 vehicles for the highway.[[12]](#cite_note-TMIS-13) At the end of the concurrency, M‑64 turns northerly to run along [Lake Gogebic](/wiki/Lake_Gogebic "Lake Gogebic").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12)

The highway continues parallel to the state line from the Marensico area through the national forest toward [Watersmeet](/wiki/Watersmeet,_Michigan "Watersmeet, Michigan"), where it crosses [US 45](/wiki/U.S._Route_45_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 45 in Michigan"). That [unincorporated community](/wiki/Unincorporated_area#United_States "Unincorporated area") is the home of the Watersmeet High School Nimrods, the basketball team featured on a series of [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") commercials and a documentary series on the [Sundance Channel](/wiki/Sundance_Channel_(United_States) "Sundance Channel (United States)").[[13]](#cite_note-espn-14) The area is also [where the waters meet](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin"); the rolling hills drain to [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") via the [Ontonagon River](/wiki/Ontonagon_River "Ontonagon River"), to [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") via the [Brule](/wiki/Brule_River "Brule River") and [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee_River "Menominee River") rivers, or to the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") via the [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin_River "Wisconsin River") and [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") rivers. Also located in the area are the [Sylvania Wilderness](/wiki/Sylvania_Wilderness "Sylvania Wilderness"), and the [Lac Vieux Desert Indian Reservation](/wiki/Lac_Vieux_Desert_Indian_Reservation "Lac Vieux Desert Indian Reservation"), which includes the Lac Vieux Desert Casino and Resort.[[14]](#cite_note-huntsWM-15) The highway travels southeasterly from Watersmeet around the many lakes and streams in the area and crosses into rural [Iron County](/wiki/Iron_County,_Michigan "Iron County, Michigan"). US 2 intersects [Federal Forest Highway 16](/wiki/Forest_Highway_16 "Forest Highway 16") (FFH 16) near Golden Lake in [Stambaugh Township](/wiki/Stambaugh_Township,_Michigan "Stambaugh Township, Michigan") in the middle of the national forest. The trunkline then runs along the [Iron River](/wiki/Iron_River_(Menominee_River_tributary) "Iron River (Menominee River tributary)") as it approaches [the city of the same name](/wiki/Iron_River,_Michigan "Iron River, Michigan") and meets [M‑73](/wiki/M-73_(Michigan_highway) "M-73 (Michigan highway)"). In town, US 2 intersects [M‑189](/wiki/M-189_(Michigan_highway) "M-189 (Michigan highway)") before crossing the river and turning northeast out of the city.[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12)

[![Photograph of the city limits sign showing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/CrystalFallsMichiganSignUS2US141.jpg/250px-CrystalFallsMichiganSignUS2US141.jpg)](/wiki/File:CrystalFallsMichiganSignUS2US141.jpg)

US 2 entering Crystal Falls as a two-lane highway

US 2 leaves the Ottawa National Forest at Iron River,[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12) and the highway continues eastward through forest lands near several small lakes to [Crystal Falls](/wiki/Crystal_Falls,_Michigan "Crystal Falls, Michigan"), the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Iron County. On the west side of town, US 2 meets [US 141](/wiki/U.S._Route_141 "U.S. Route 141"); the two highways run concurrently along Crystal Avenue. The combined highway turns south onto 5th Street and meets [M‑69](/wiki/M-69_(Michigan_highway) "M-69 (Michigan highway)")'s eastern terminus at the intersection between 5th Street and Superior Avenue next to the [county courthouse](/wiki/Iron_County_Courthouse_(Crystal_Falls,_Michigan) "Iron County Courthouse (Crystal Falls, Michigan)") at the top of the hill. US 2/US 141 runs south out of Crystal Falls to the west of, and parallel to, the Paint River. The roadway passes Railroad, Kennedy and Stager lakes and leaves the state of Michigan at the Brule River,[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9) crossing into [Florence County, Wisconsin](/wiki/Florence_County,_Wisconsin "Florence County, Wisconsin") for about 14 miles (23 km).[[7]](#cite_note-WisDOT-8)

### Eastern segment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Eastern segment")]

US 2/US 141 re-enters Michigan where it crosses the Menominee River and subsequently meets [M‑95](/wiki/M-95_(Michigan_highway) "M-95 (Michigan highway)") in [Breitung Township](/wiki/Breitung_Township,_Michigan "Breitung Township, Michigan") north of [Iron Mountain](/wiki/Iron_Mountain,_Michigan "Iron Mountain, Michigan") and [Kingsford](/wiki/Kingsford,_Michigan "Kingsford, Michigan"). The highways merge in a triple concurrency and run south on Stephenson Avenue into Iron Mountain along the west side of Lake Antoine, parallel to a branch line of the [Escanaba and Lake Superior Railroad](/wiki/Escanaba_and_Lake_Superior_Railroad "Escanaba and Lake Superior Railroad") (ELS Railroad).[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)[[15]](#cite_note-MDOT-RR-16) The road crosses through a retail corridor and over a flooded pit of the Chapin Mine. In downtown Iron Mountain at Ludington Street, M‑95 turns west off Stephenson Avenue to run across town to Kingsford. US 2/US 141 exits downtown and turns east along a second retail corridor near the Midtown Mall. The highway re-enters Breitung Township where US 141 separates to the south to re-enter Wisconsin.[[8]](#cite_note-google-9) US 2 continues eastward parallel to a branch of the [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway") (CN Railway).[[15]](#cite_note-MDOT-RR-16) Both road and rail travel through the community of [Quinnesec](/wiki/Quinnesec,_Michigan "Quinnesec, Michigan"), where they pass near the largest paper mill in the UP.[[16]](#cite_note-huntsIM-17) The trunkline runs along the main street of [Norway](/wiki/Norway,_Michigan "Norway, Michigan"), where the highway meets the eastern terminus of [US 8](/wiki/U.S._Route_8 "U.S. Route 8"). Then US 2 continues east through rural [Dickinson County](/wiki/Dickinson_County,_Michigan "Dickinson County, Michigan") to [Vulcan](/wiki/Vulcan,_Michigan "Vulcan, Michigan"), passing north of Hanbury Lake through the [Copper Country State Forest](/wiki/Copper_Country_State_Forest "Copper Country State Forest"), before crossing the [Sturgeon River](/w/index.php?title=Sturgeon_River_(Dickinson_County,_Michigan)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sturgeon River (Dickinson County, Michigan) (page does not exist)") in [Loretto](/wiki/Loretto,_Michigan "Loretto, Michigan") and passing into [Menominee County](/wiki/Menominee_County,_Michigan "Menominee County, Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)

[![Photograph showing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/IronMountainMichiganDowntownUS2US141.jpg/250px-IronMountainMichiganDowntownUS2US141.jpg)](/wiki/File:IronMountainMichiganDowntownUS2US141.jpg)

US 2/US 141 at the intersection with East B Street, downtown Iron Mountain

In Menominee County, the environment takes on a more agricultural character along US 2. The highway passes through the edge of the community of [Hermansville](/wiki/Hermansville,_Michigan "Hermansville, Michigan") before entering [Powers](/wiki/Powers,_Michigan "Powers, Michigan"). US 2 comes to a three-way intersection and turns northeast merging onto [US 41](/wiki/U.S._Route_41_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 41 in Michigan"). The concurrent highway runs from Powers through the communities of [Wilson](/wiki/Wilson,_Michigan "Wilson, Michigan") and [Spaulding](/w/index.php?title=Spaulding,_Michigan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Spaulding, Michigan (page does not exist)") on the south side of the CN Railway. At [Harris](/wiki/Harris,_Michigan "Harris, Michigan"), the trunkline enters the [Hannahville Indian Community](/wiki/Hannahville_Indian_Community "Hannahville Indian Community"). Harris is on the Menominee County side of the reservation, but as the highway continues east, it crosses over to [Bark River](/wiki/Bark_River,_Michigan "Bark River, Michigan") on the [Delta County](/wiki/Delta_County,_Michigan "Delta County, Michigan") side. The county line in between not only separates the two communities, but also serves as the boundary between the [Central](/wiki/Central_Time_Zone_(North_America) "Central Time Zone (North America)") and [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") time zones. East of Bark River, the highway crosses [the community's namesake waterway](/wiki/Bark_River_(Michigan) "Bark River (Michigan)") before intersecting the eastern terminus of M‑69. The roadway crosses the [Ford River](/wiki/Ford_River_(Michigan) "Ford River (Michigan)") prior to turning due east into the outskirts of [Escanaba](/wiki/Escanaba,_Michigan "Escanaba, Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)

US 2/US 41 widens to four lanes along Ludington Street, which forms the east–west axis of the Escanaba street grid. Near downtown, the highway meets [M‑35](/wiki/M-35_(Michigan_highway) "M-35 (Michigan highway)"), which runs along the city's north–south axis, Lincoln Road. The trunklines merge and run north, bypassing the traditional central business district for a different business corridor.[[8]](#cite_note-google-9) Lincoln Road runs north carrying four lanes of traffic past the [Upper Peninsula State Fairgrounds](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_State_Fair "Upper Peninsula State Fair"), site of one of the two [state fairs](/wiki/State_fair "State fair") for the state of Michigan, the only state to have twin fairs.[[17]](#cite_note-huntsE-18) US 2/US 41/M‑35 continues north on Lincoln Road past the campus of [Bay de Noc Community College](/wiki/Bay_de_Noc_Community_College "Bay de Noc Community College"). The four-lane highway crosses the [Escanaba River](/wiki/Escanaba_River "Escanaba River") just upstream from its mouth near the large Verso Esky Paper Mill and shifts to run immediately next to [Little Bay de Noc](/wiki/Little_Bay_de_Noc "Little Bay de Noc").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)[[17]](#cite_note-huntsE-18) The section here carried the highest traffic counts along all of US 2 in the state: an average of 23,977 vehicles used this segment of roadway daily in 2011.[[12]](#cite_note-TMIS-13)

[![Photograph showing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/US2_and_US41_Expressway_north_of_Gladstone_Michigan.jpg/250px-US2_and_US41_Expressway_north_of_Gladstone_Michigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:US2_and_US41_Expressway_north_of_Gladstone_Michigan.jpg)

The US 2/US 41 expressway north of Gladstone was, until 2017, the only non-freeway section of highway in the UP with a speed limit higher than 55 mph (89 km/h).

The road turns inland again, and US 2/US 41/M‑35 passes to the west of downtown [Gladstone](/wiki/Gladstone,_Michigan "Gladstone, Michigan"). The highway through here is an [expressway](/wiki/Limited-access_road "Limited-access road"), four lanes divided by a central median and no driveway access. Unlike a [freeway](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway"), the expressway has standard [intersections](/wiki/Intersection_(road) "Intersection (road)") and not [interchanges](/wiki/Interchange_(road) "Interchange (road)"). The highway intersects the eastern terminus of County Road 426 (CR 426) and crosses the ELS Railroad south of the stoplight for 4th Avenue North, where M‑35 separates from the US Highways and turns to the northwest. The expressway continues north parallel to the CN Railway, crossing the [Days River](/wiki/Days_River "Days River").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9) From Gladstone to [St. Ignace](/wiki/Saint_Ignace,_Michigan "Saint Ignace, Michigan"), US 2 carries a speed limit of 65 mph (105 km/h) for all traffic.[[18]](#cite_note-which-19) This was, before 2017, the only road in the UP with a speed limit higher than 55 mph (89 km/h) besides [I-75](/wiki/Interstate_75_in_Michigan "Interstate 75 in Michigan"), which has a speed limit of 75 mph (121 km/h).[[19]](#cite_note-lancour-20) The expressway segment runs around the upper end of Little Bay de Noc before ending at [Rapid River](/wiki/Rapid_River,_Michigan "Rapid River, Michigan"). In this location, US 41 separates to the north, and US 2 returns to an easterly track as a two-lane road, crossing the [Rapid](/wiki/Rapid_River_(Delta_County,_Michigan) "Rapid River (Delta County, Michigan)") and [Whitefish](/wiki/Whitefish_River_(Michigan) "Whitefish River (Michigan)") rivers and turning southeast around the head of the bay.[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7) As US 2 crosses southern Delta County, it passes through the western unit of the [Hiawatha National Forest](/wiki/Hiawatha_National_Forest "Hiawatha National Forest").[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12) Near [Garden Corners](/wiki/Garden_Corners,_Michigan "Garden Corners, Michigan"), the highway runs along the shore of [Big Bay de Noc](/wiki/Big_Bay_de_Noc "Big Bay de Noc"). After the intersection with the northern terminus of [M‑183](/wiki/M-183_(Michigan_highway) "M-183 (Michigan highway)"), US 2 turns inland cutting across the base of the [Garden Peninsula](/wiki/Garden_Peninsula "Garden Peninsula") and enters [Schoolcraft County](/wiki/Schoolcraft_County,_Michigan "Schoolcraft County, Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)

As the highway approaches [Thompson](/wiki/Thompson,_Michigan "Thompson, Michigan"), US 2 leaves the western unit of the Hiawatha National Forest and enters the [Lake Superior State Forest](/wiki/Lake_Superior_State_Forest "Lake Superior State Forest").[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12) The roadway runs along [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") to [Manistique](/wiki/Manistique,_Michigan "Manistique, Michigan"), crossing the [Manistique River](/wiki/Manistique_River "Manistique River"). The trunkline turns inland approaching [Gulliver](/wiki/Gulliver,_Michigan "Gulliver, Michigan") and then turns north-northeast to [Blaney Park](/wiki/Blaney_Park,_Michigan "Blaney Park, Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7) The community of Blaney Park is a former logging town-turned-resort at the southern terminus of [M‑77](/wiki/M-77_(Michigan_highway) "M-77 (Michigan highway)"); the resort was active from the late 1920s but declined by the 1980s.[[20]](#cite_note-huntsBP-21) From Blaney Park, US 2 turns due east and crosses into [Mackinac County](/wiki/Mackinac_County,_Michigan "Mackinac County, Michigan") west of [Gould City](/wiki/Gould_City,_Michigan "Gould City, Michigan"). Where it intersects a former routing, the main highway crosses the CN Railway one last time and runs to the south of [Engadine](/wiki/Engadine,_Michigan "Engadine, Michigan") to follow the Lake Michigan shoreline through [Naubinway](/wiki/Naubinway,_Michigan "Naubinway, Michigan").[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7) After passing the community of [Epoufette](/wiki/Epoufette,_Michigan "Epoufette, Michigan"),[[8]](#cite_note-google-9) US 2 crosses the [Cut River Bridge](/wiki/Cut_River_Bridge "Cut River Bridge"), 147 feet (45 m) over the [Cut River](/wiki/Cut_River_(Mackinac_County,_Michigan) "Cut River (Mackinac County, Michigan)").[[21]](#cite_note-batchelder-22) The highway crosses into the eastern unit of the Hiawatha National Forest near [Brevort](/wiki/Brevort,_Michigan "Brevort, Michigan"), running between Lake Michigan and [Brevoort Lake](/wiki/Brevoort_Lake "Brevoort Lake") in the process.[[11]](#cite_note-RMN08-12) The road continues along the Lake Michigan shoreline, passing Mystery Spot near [Gros Cap](/wiki/Gros_Cap,_Michigan "Gros Cap, Michigan") and turning inland immediately west of St. Ignace. The US 2 designation ends at the highway's [partial cloverleaf interchange](/wiki/Partial_cloverleaf_interchange "Partial cloverleaf interchange") with I‑75. The roadway continues easterly into downtown St. Ignace as [Business Loop I‑75](/wiki/Interstate_75_Business_(St._Ignace,_Michigan) "Interstate 75 Business (St. Ignace, Michigan)") (BL I‑75).[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7)[[8]](#cite_note-google-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

### Indian trail through auto trails

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Indian trail through auto trails")]

[![Cloverland Trail marker from 1915](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Cloverland_Trail.svg/120px-Cloverland_Trail.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cloverland_Trail.svg)

[![Theodore Roosevelt International Highway marker from 1919](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Theodore_Roosevelt_International_Highway_marker.svg/120px-Theodore_Roosevelt_International_Highway_marker.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Theodore_Roosevelt_International_Highway_marker.svg)

Highway markers for the [Cloverland Trail](/wiki/Cloverland_Trail "Cloverland Trail") (left) and Theodore Roosevelt International Highway (right), both of which predate the modern highway

In 1701, the first transportation routes through what became the state of Michigan were the lakes, rivers and Indian trails. Two of these trails followed parts of the future US 2. The Sault–Green Bay Trail roughly followed the Lake Michigan shoreline routing of US 2 between [Escanaba](/wiki/Escanaba,_Michigan "Escanaba, Michigan") and [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace,_Michigan "St. Ignace, Michigan"). The [Mackinac Trail](/wiki/Mackinac_Trail "Mackinac Trail") connected St. Ignace with Sault Ste. Marie.[[22]](#cite_note-Mason-23)

In the age of the [auto trail](/wiki/Auto_trail "Auto trail"), the roads that later formed US 2 through the UP were given a few different highway names. When the original roadways between Ironwood and Iron River were completed in late 1915, the Upper Peninsula Development Bureau (UPDB) named the area Cloverland and the highway the [Cloverland Trail](/wiki/Cloverland_Trail "Cloverland Trail"). Later the name was extended over the highway to Escanaba, and to all highways in the area in the early 1920s; the name was phased out by the UPDB completely in 1927.[[23]](#cite_note-barnett57-8-24) The roadways were also used for the [Theodore Roosevelt International Highway](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_International_Highway "Theodore Roosevelt International Highway"), named for former US president [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") after his death in 1919. Overall, this highway ran from [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), to [Portland, Maine](/wiki/Portland,_Maine "Portland, Maine"), by way of Michigan and the Canadian province of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"). Through the UP, the southern branch followed the immediate predecessors to US 2, including the section through Florence County, Wisconsin.[[24]](#cite_note-barnett211-25)[[b]](#cite_note-26)

The Great Lakes Automobile Route was established in 1917 by the UPDB. A predecessor of the [Great Lakes Circle Tours](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Circle_Tour "Great Lakes Circle Tour") by seventy years, the route followed "a circular journey along the banks of lakes Michigan and Superior and Green Bay ..."[[25]](#cite_note-barnett96-27) This route followed the modern US 2 from Ironwood to the M‑94 junction in Manistique, using the modern M‑69 and M‑95 to stay in Michigan. Branches of the route followed US 41 and M‑35 between Powers and Escanaba. The route was originally intended to entice motorists to drive around Lake Michigan. The name fell out of use before its first anniversary because of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[25]](#cite_note-barnett96-27)

One Canadian auto trail was routed through the UP as well. In 1920, the King's International Highway linked [Vancouver, British Columbia](/wiki/Vancouver,_British_Columbia "Vancouver, British Columbia"), to [Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax_Regional_Municipality "Halifax Regional Municipality"), but there was no highway to carry it around the north side of Lake Superior. Motorists had to ship their cars by boat between [Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Ontario "Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario"), and [Thunder Bay](/wiki/Thunder_Bay,_Ontario "Thunder Bay, Ontario") or enter the United States to continue along the auto trail. The routings varied on the maps of the time, but its basic route used US 2 through the UP from Ironwood to Sault Ste. Marie until a highway north of Lake Superior was opened in 1960; by that time, the auto trail had taken on the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway") name.[[26]](#cite_note-barnett127-28)

### State trunkline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: State trunkline")]

The first state trunkline highway designated along the path of the modern US 2 was [M‑12](/wiki/M-12_(Michigan_highway) "M-12 (Michigan highway)"), a designation that was in use by July 1, 1919, between Ironwood and [Sault Ste. Marie](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Michigan "Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan").[[27]](#cite_note-MSHD19UP-29)[[c]](#cite_note-name-31) The first roadside park in the country was created by Herbert Larson near what is now US 2 near [Iron River](/wiki/Iron_River,_Michigan "Iron River, Michigan") in 1919–20.[[29]](#cite_note-32) When the US Highway System was created on November 11, 1926,[[2]](#cite_note-mcnichol74-3) US 2 partially replaced M-12.[[30]](#cite_note-USHM-33) Between Crystal Falls and Iron Mountain, US 2 was routed through [Florence, Wisconsin](/wiki/Florence_(CDP),_Wisconsin "Florence (CDP), Wisconsin"). The former routing of M‑12 from Crystal Falls to Sagola became a new [M‑69](/wiki/M-69_(Michigan_highway) "M-69 (Michigan highway)") when the former M‑69 became US 102 (now US 141). M‑12 from Sagola south to Iron Mountain was made a part of an extended [M‑45](/wiki/M-45_(Michigan_highway) "M-45 (Michigan highway)"), which is now [M‑95](/wiki/M-95_(Michigan_highway) "M-95 (Michigan highway)"). By the next year, [M‑48](/wiki/M-48_(Michigan_highway) "M-48 (Michigan highway)") was added along US 2 from [Rexton](/wiki/Rexton,_Michigan "Rexton, Michigan") to [Garnet](/wiki/Garnet,_Michigan "Garnet, Michigan") as part of a larger extension.[[31]](#cite_note-MSHD27-12-34)

The first changes to the routing of US 2 itself were made in 1930 with a bypass of downtown Escanaba.[[32]](#cite_note-MSHD30-01-35)[[33]](#cite_note-MSHD30-07-36) A larger rerouting was completed in 1933 between [Rogers Park](/wiki/Rogers_Park,_Michigan "Rogers Park, Michigan") and Sault Ste. Marie. The new routing followed Mackinac Trail instead of turning east to [Cedarville](/wiki/Cedarville,_Michigan "Cedarville, Michigan") and north to Sault Ste. Marie; the former routing was given the [M‑121](/wiki/M-121_(Michigan_highway) "M-121 (Michigan highway)") designation.[[34]](#cite_note-MSHD33-05-37)[[35]](#cite_note-MSHD33-09-38) Another realignment in the Iron Mountain area shifted US 2/US 141 to a new bridge over the [Menominee River](/wiki/Menominee_River "Menominee River") between 1932 and 1934.[[36]](#cite_note-MSHD32-04-39)[[37]](#cite_note-MSHD34-09-40) Downtown Ironwood was bypassed in 1934, and the former route was initially designated [M‑54](/wiki/M-54_(Michigan_highway) "M-54 (Michigan highway)").[[35]](#cite_note-MSHD33-09-38)[[37]](#cite_note-MSHD34-09-40)

[![Map of the](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/US_2_near_Manistique_in_1936.jpg/250px-US_2_near_Manistique_in_1936.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_2_near_Manistique_in_1936.jpg)

The Manistique area in mid-1936; US 2, denoted by a thick red line, runs east–west through the area north of its current routing, which is shown as a dashed line.

The [Michigan State Highway Department](/wiki/Michigan_State_Highway_Department "Michigan State Highway Department") (MSHD)[[d]](#cite_note-MDOT-43) changed the routings and designations of the highways around [Cooks](/wiki/Cooks,_Michigan "Cooks, Michigan"), [Thompson](/wiki/Thompson,_Michigan "Thompson, Michigan") and [Manistique](/wiki/Manistique,_Michigan "Manistique, Michigan") in the mid-1930s. The agency rerouted US 2 between Cooks and [M‑149](/wiki/M-149_(Michigan_highway) "M-149 (Michigan highway)") in Thompson, turning the old road back to county control. The section between M‑149 and [M‑125](/wiki/M-125_(Michigan_highway) "M-125 (Michigan highway)") was redesignated as an extension of M‑149 to Thompson, and M‑125 was replaced by a further extension of M‑149. The last change was to route US 2 along its current alignment in the area, completing the changes on August 2, 1936.[[40]](#cite_note-MSHD36-12-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45)

The MSHD started construction in 1936 on a new road that rerouted US 2 into St. Ignace for the first time. Between [Brevort](/wiki/Brevort,_Michigan "Brevort, Michigan") and [Moran](/wiki/Moran,_Michigan "Moran, Michigan"), US 2 previously followed Worth Road inland to the Tahquamenon Trail to meet the northern extension of [US 31](/wiki/U.S._Route_31_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 31 in Michigan") into the Upper Peninsula.[[42]](#cite_note-MSHD36-06-46) The new routing took US 2 along the lakeshore into St. Ignace. US 31 was truncated to the state ferry docks in [Mackinaw City](/wiki/Mackinaw_City,_Michigan "Mackinaw City, Michigan") and US 2 was routed through St. Ignace along the former US 31 to Rogers Park; the connection in St. Ignace to the state ferry docks became [M‑122](/wiki/M-122_(Michigan_highway) "M-122 (Michigan highway)").[[40]](#cite_note-MSHD36-12-44) Further changes in the early 1940s straightened the roadway out near Watersmeet and Crystal Falls.[[43]](#cite_note-MSHD39-12-47)[[44]](#cite_note-MSHD41-07-48)

Additional realignments were completed by the MSHD to move US 2 to its modern lakeshore routing between [Gould City](/wiki/Gould_City,_Michigan "Gould City, Michigan") and [Epoufette](/wiki/Epoufette,_Michigan "Epoufette, Michigan") in 1941. The new highway traveled due east from Gould City to [Naubinway](/wiki/Naubinway,_Michigan "Naubinway, Michigan") and then along the lake to Epoufette. The former route through Engadine was turned back to local control as far east as [Garnet](/wiki/Garnet,_Michigan "Garnet, Michigan"). From there east, it was numbered just [M‑48](/wiki/M-48_(Michigan_highway) "M-48 (Michigan highway)"), removing US 2 from a concurrency. Another former section into Epoufette was added to extend [M‑117](/wiki/M-117_(Michigan_highway) "M-117 (Michigan highway)").[[45]](#cite_note-MSHD42-06-49) The new highway was detoured around the [Cut River Bridge](/wiki/Cut_River_Bridge "Cut River Bridge") until it was completed in 1946 after construction delays over steel shortages during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[46]](#cite_note-hyde-50)[[47]](#cite_note-MSHD45-10-51)

The western end of US 2 took on two changes in the 1940s. [M‑28](/wiki/M-28_(Michigan_highway) "M-28 (Michigan highway)") was extended along US 2 to the state line at Ironwood from its western terminus at Wakefield.[[45]](#cite_note-MSHD42-06-49) A similar extension was made from M‑28's eastern terminus to Sault Ste. Marie in 1948.[[48]](#cite_note-MSHD48-07-52) The M‑54 designation was renumbered as [Business US 2](/wiki/U.S._Route_2_Business_(Ironwood,_Michigan) "U.S. Route 2 Business (Ironwood, Michigan)") by 1945.[[47]](#cite_note-MSHD45-10-51) The eastern M‑28 extension was reversed in 1950,[[49]](#cite_note-MSHD50-04-53) and the western extension to the state line was shifted to a new location by 1952.[[50]](#cite_note-MSHD52-04-54)

### Interstate era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Interstate era")]

[![Photograph looking north along I‑75 showing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/US_Route_2_Western_Segment_Eastern_Terminus.JPG/250px-US_Route_2_Western_Segment_Eastern_Terminus.JPG)](/wiki/File:US_Route_2_Western_Segment_Eastern_Terminus.JPG)

The eastern terminus of US 2 in St. Ignace; until 1983 US 2 continued north of here along the I‑75 freeway.

With the coming of the [Interstate Highway System](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") in Michigan, the MSHD planned to convert the eastern section of US 2 to a freeway between St. Ignace and Sault Ste. Marie. In planning maps from 1947, this highway corridor was included in the system that later became the Interstates.[[51]](#cite_note-NSHIS47-55) It was also included in the *General Location of National System of Interstate Highways Including All Additional Routes at Urban Areas Designated in September 1955*, or *Yellow Book* after the cover color, that was released in 1955 as the federal government readied plans for the freeway system.[[52]](#cite_note-YBD-56) The proposed number in 1958 was [Interstate 75](/wiki/Interstate_75_in_Michigan "Interstate 75 in Michigan") (I‑75).[[53]](#cite_note-RINRM58-57)

The first section of freeway was built in late 1957 or early 1958 between [Evergreen Shores](/wiki/Evergreen_Shores,_Michigan "Evergreen Shores, Michigan") and [M‑123](/wiki/M-123_(Michigan_highway) "M-123 (Michigan highway)") north of St. Ignace.[[54]](#cite_note-MSHD57-10-58)[[55]](#cite_note-MSHD58-59) The [Mackinac Bridge](/wiki/Mackinac_Bridge "Mackinac Bridge") was opened to traffic on November 1, 1957;[[56]](#cite_note-kulsea-60) a new section of freeway and an [interchange](/wiki/Interchange_(road) "Interchange (road)") connected US 2 to the bridge.[[57]](#cite_note-61) In 1961, another new freeway segment closed the gap between the Mackinac Bridge and Evergreen Shores sections. At the time, the I‑75 designation supplanted [US 27](/wiki/U.S._Route_27_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 27 in Michigan") on the bridge, and US 2 was shifted to follow I‑75 along the freeways in the St. Ignace area. The former routing of US 2 in downtown St. Ignace was redesignated [BL I‑75](/wiki/Interstate_75_Business_(St._Ignace,_Michigan) "Interstate 75 Business (St. Ignace, Michigan)").[[58]](#cite_note-MSHD61-62) More sections of freeway were opened in 1962 immediately to the south of the newly constructed [International Bridge](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie_International_Bridge "Sault Ste. Marie International Bridge") in Sault Ste. Marie as well as between [Dafter](/wiki/Dafter,_Michigan "Dafter, Michigan") and [Kinross](/wiki/Kinross,_Michigan "Kinross, Michigan").[[59]](#cite_note-MSHD62-63)[[60]](#cite_note-MSHD63-64) The last two sections opened in 1963 connected the northern end of the freeway at M‑123 to Kinross, and the section between Dafter and Sault Ste. Marie. At this time, all of US 2's former routing became a [county road known as Mackinac Trail (H-63)](/wiki/H-63_(Michigan_county_highway) "H-63 (Michigan county highway)").[[60]](#cite_note-MSHD63-64)[[61]](#cite_note-MSHD64-65)

The [Department of State Highways](/wiki/Michigan_Department_of_State_Highways "Michigan Department of State Highways")[[d]](#cite_note-MDOT-43) expanded US 2/US 41 into an expressway between [Gladstone](/wiki/Gladstone,_Michigan "Gladstone, Michigan") and [Rapid River](/wiki/Rapid_River,_Michigan "Rapid River, Michigan") in 1971.[[62]](#cite_note-MDSH71-66)[[63]](#cite_note-MDSH72-67) The state built a new bridge over the [Manistique River](/wiki/Manistique_River "Manistique River") in 1983, bypassing downtown. MDOT disposed of the former routing of US 2 into downtown in two ways. The western half was initially an unnumbered state highway until it was later transferred to local control. An extension of [M‑94](/wiki/M-94_(Michigan_highway) "M-94 (Michigan highway)") replaced the remainder, including the Siphon Bridge, through downtown. In that same year, the department truncated US 2 to end in St. Ignace by removing it from the I‑75 freeway.[[64]](#cite_note-MDOT83-68)[[65]](#cite_note-MDOT84-69) The last changes were made to US 2's routing through Iron River in 1998, bypassing the bridge that formerly carried the highway over the river in town.[[66]](#cite_note-mdot-IR-70) In 2011, MDOT raised the speed limit along the expressway section in Delta County from 55 to 65 mph (89 to 105 km/h), although the speed limit for trucks remained 55 mph (89 km/h)[[19]](#cite_note-lancour-20) until 2017. That year the highway's speed limits were raised to 65 mph (105 km/h) between Wakefield and Iron River as well as between Rapid River and St. Ignace.[[18]](#cite_note-which-19) In 2020, MDOT announced the slight relocation of US 2 in Mackinac County just west of the Cut River Bridge due to [sinkholes](/wiki/Sinkhole "Sinkhole") and [shoreline erosion](/wiki/Shoreline_erosion "Shoreline erosion") on Lake Michigan near the roadway.[[67]](#cite_note-71)

## Memorial designations and tourist routes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Memorial designations and tourist routes")]

On July 1, 1924, the [State Administrative Board](/wiki/Michigan_State_Administrative_Board "Michigan State Administrative Board") named M‑12, the predecessor to US 2 in Michigan, the Bohn Highway to honor [Frank P. Bohn](/wiki/Frank_P._Bohn "Frank P. Bohn"), a prominent local citizen who later served in [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") from 1927 to 1933.[[68]](#cite_note-barnett36-7-72) In 1929, the residents of Escanaba created a memorial to the veterans of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") called Memory Lane. The project consisted of [elm](/wiki/Elm "Elm") and [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple") trees planted along US 2/US 41 west of town. The [American Legion](/wiki/American_Legion "American Legion") sold the trees to local businesses and individuals who could honor specific soldiers.[[69]](#cite_note-barnett151-73) Later in 1949, the Bessemer Women's Club created a tribute in the form of a permanent living memorial to the area veterans. Also called Memory Lane, the group planted 140 elms and 1,840 evergreens, trees and shrubs as a landscaped parkway along 2.3 miles (3.7 km) of US 2 east of Bessemer.[[70]](#cite_note-barnett149-74)

Most of US 2, along with [US 23](/wiki/U.S._Route_23_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 23 in Michigan") in the [Lower Peninsula](/wiki/Lower_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Lower Peninsula of Michigan"), was designated the United Spanish War Veterans Memorial Highway in 1949. To connect the gap in the routing where US 2 cuts through Wisconsin, M‑95 and M‑69 were used in place of US 2 between Iron Mountain and Crystal Falls. Signs marking the highway were not erected until 1968 when Governor [George W. Romney](/wiki/George_W._Romney "George W. Romney") had them installed.[[71]](#cite_note-barnett216-7-75)

The Amvets Memorial Drive designation was created for the section of US 2/US 41/M‑35 between the northern Escanaba city limits and County Road 426 (CR 426) in Delta County. The [American Veterans](/wiki/AMVETS "AMVETS") (AMVETS) organization in Michigan petitioned the [Michigan Legislature](/wiki/Michigan_Legislature "Michigan Legislature") to grant this designation, which was assigned under Public Act 144 in 1959.[[72]](#cite_note-barnett24-76)

[![Photograph of showing the](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/US2_Along_Lake_Michigan.jpg/250px-US2_Along_Lake_Michigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:US2_Along_Lake_Michigan.jpg)

View of Lake Michigan near Brevort; the highway runs along the lake from Naubinway to its eastern terminus at St. Ignace.

Two sections of US 2 are part of the overall [Great Lakes Circle Tour](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Circle_Tour "Great Lakes Circle Tour") (GLCT): the segment from the Wisconsin state line near Ironwood to the M‑28 junction in Wakefield is part of the [Lake Superior Circle Tour](/wiki/Lake_Superior_Circle_Tour "Lake Superior Circle Tour") (LSCT), and the segment from the southern M‑35 junction in Escanaba to the eastern terminus in St. Ignace is part of the [Lake Michigan Circle Tour](/wiki/Lake_Michigan_Circle_Tour "Lake Michigan Circle Tour") (LMCT).[[6]](#cite_note-MDOT10-7) These two tours were created in May 1986 through a joint effort between MDOT and its counterparts in Wisconsin, [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") and Ontario.[[73]](#cite_note-davis1986-05-01-77) The section of US 2 between Iron River and Crystal Falls has been named the Iron County Heritage Trail. This [Pure Michigan Byway](/wiki/Pure_Michigan_Byway "Pure Michigan Byway") was designated to honor the "rich history of two industries that built a state and nation: mining and logging."[[74]](#cite_note-ICHT-78) On August 26, 2007, MDOT announced that the section of US 2 that runs concurrently with M‑35 in Delta County was being included in the [UP Hidden Coast Recreational Heritage Trail](/wiki/UP_Hidden_Coast_Recreational_Heritage_Trail "UP Hidden Coast Recreational Heritage Trail").[[75]](#cite_note-hunts-gb-79)[[76]](#cite_note-tv6-2-80) The segment between [Thompson](/wiki/Thompson,_Michigan "Thompson, Michigan") and [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace,_Michigan "St. Ignace, Michigan") along the northern shore of Lake Michigan was designated the Top of the Lake Scenic Byway in the Pure Michigan Byways program on October 9, 2017.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

## Historic bridges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Historic bridges")]

There are six bridges along current or former sections of US 2 that MDOT has added to its listing of Michigan's Historic Bridges; [two of these](/wiki/List_of_bridges_on_the_National_Register_of_Historic_Places_in_Michigan "List of bridges on the National Register of Historic Places in Michigan") are also listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") (NRHP). A third bridge added to the NRHP in late 2012 has not been added to MDOT's listing however. The first of these historic bridges is the [crossing of the Iron River](/wiki/US_2%E2%80%93Iron_River_Bridge "US 2–Iron River Bridge"),[[66]](#cite_note-mdot-IR-70) which has since been bypassed by a new bridge.[[78]](#cite_note-82) The original structure, dating to 1918, is a 55-foot-long (17 m) [spandrel arch](/wiki/Spandrel_arch "Spandrel arch") span that was built by the MSHD as Trunk Line Bridge No. 191.[[66]](#cite_note-mdot-IR-70) The structure was listed on the NRHP on December 9, 1999, for its architectural and engineering significance.[[79]](#cite_note-NRIS-83)

In December 2012, the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") approved the listing of the [Upper Twin Falls Bridge](/wiki/Upper_Twin_Falls_Bridge "Upper Twin Falls Bridge") that crosses the Menominee River northwest of Iron Mountain. The structure is a single-span, pin-connected, [camelback](/wiki/Camelback_truss "Camelback truss"), [through-truss](/wiki/Through-truss "Through-truss") bridge, and it is the only known example of its type in Michigan. It was built between 1909 and 1910 because the Twin Falls Power Dam would flood an existing river crossing. The span cost $5,106 (equivalent to $124,000 in 2024[[80]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-84)), paid equally by [Dickinson](/wiki/Dickinson_County,_Michigan "Dickinson County, Michigan") and [Florence](/wiki/Florence_County,_Wisconsin "Florence County, Wisconsin") counties.[[81]](#cite_note-DailyNews2012-85) Until the 1930s, the Upper Twin Falls Bridge carried US 2 across the Menominee River.[[82]](#cite_note-map-86) In 1934, a new bridge was built about a mile downstream, and the highway was rerouted over the new span.[[83]](#cite_note-DailyNews-87) The bridge closed to automobile traffic in September 1971,[[84]](#cite_note-DailyNews2013-88) and the nomination process for inclusion on the National Register of Historic Places began in 2012.[[81]](#cite_note-DailyNews2012-85)

In 2003, MDOT replaced the Sturgeon River Bridge in [Waucedah Township](/wiki/Waucedah_Township,_Michigan "Waucedah Township, Michigan"), Dickinson County.[[85]](#cite_note-SRBPR-89) As of October 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit), even though the old bridge was demolished and replaced, MDOT retained it on their historic bridge list. It was built in 1929.[[86]](#cite_note-mdot-SR-90)

Before 1983, US 2 used a different routing through Manistique and crossed the Manistique River on what is nicknamed the "Siphon Bridge". Built as a part of a raceway flume on the river, the water level is actually higher than the road surface. This produces a [siphon](/wiki/Siphon "Siphon") effect, giving the bridge its nickname. The Manistique Pulp and Paper Company was organized in 1916 and needed a dam on the Manistique River to supply their mill. This dam would require a large section of the city to be flooded, and shallow river banks meant difficulties in any bridge construction. Instead of expensive dikes, a concrete tank was built lengthwise in the river bed; the sides of this tank provided man-made banks higher than the natural banks. The Michigan [Works Progress Administration](/wiki/Works_Progress_Administration "Works Progress Administration") described the bridge as having "concrete bulkheads, formed by the side spans of the bridge, [that] allow the mill to maintain the water level several feet above the roadbed."[[87]](#cite_note-hunts-SB-91) The Manistique Tourism Council stated: "At one time, the bridge itself was partially supported by the water that was atmospherically forced under it," and that the bridge has been featured in *[Ripley's Believe It or Not!](/wiki/Ripley%27s_Believe_It_or_Not! "Ripley's Believe It or Not!")*.[[88]](#cite_note-mdotrhf-92) The eight-span structure is 294 feet (90 m) long.[[89]](#cite_note-hyde132-93)

[![Photograph of the](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Cut_River_Bridge_4.JPG/250px-Cut_River_Bridge_4.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cut_River_Bridge_4.JPG)

Cut River Bridge as seen from a nearby walkway

The [Cut River Bridge](/wiki/Cut_River_Bridge "Cut River Bridge") carries US 2 across the [Cut River](/wiki/Cut_River_(Mackinac_County,_Michigan) "Cut River (Mackinac County, Michigan)") in [Hendricks Township](/wiki/Hendricks_Township,_Michigan "Hendricks Township, Michigan"), Mackinac County. This structure was built during World War II but completion was delayed due to war-induced steel shortages.[[46]](#cite_note-hyde-50) The span uses 888 short tons (793 long tons; 806 t) of structural steel to bridge the 641 feet (195 m) over the river and its gorge at a height of 147 feet (45 m) above the river. The Cut River Bridge is one of only two [cantilevered deck truss bridges](/wiki/Cantilever_bridge "Cantilever bridge") in the state.[[90]](#cite_note-mdot-CR-94)[[e]](#cite_note-95) On either side of the bridge, there are picnic areas and trails down to the river.[[91]](#cite_note-hunts-CRB-96)

Listed on the NRHP on December 17, 1999,[[79]](#cite_note-NRIS-83) the [Mackinac Trail–Carp River Bridge](/wiki/Mackinac_Trail%E2%80%93Carp_River_Bridge "Mackinac Trail–Carp River Bridge") carries [H-63](/wiki/H-63_(Michigan_county_highway) "H-63 (Michigan county highway)"), the modern successor to US 2, over the [Carp River](/wiki/Carp_River_(Mackinac_County) "Carp River (Mackinac County)") north of St. Ignace. The bridge is another spandrel arch structure 60 feet (18 m) in length and built in 1920. Increasing traffic along Mackinac Trail prompted the MSHD to "widen its deck by five feet [1.5 m] and install new guardrails in the 1929–1930 biennium" along with the addition of decorative retaining walls.[[92]](#cite_note-mdot-MTCR-97)

The last of the historic bridges along a former segment of US 2 is the structure carrying Ashmun Street ([BS I‑75](/wiki/Interstate_75_Business_(Sault_Ste._Marie,_Michigan) "Interstate 75 Business (Sault Ste. Marie, Michigan)")) over the Power Canal in Sault Ste. Marie. Built in 1934, it is one of only three steel arch bridges in the state.[[93]](#cite_note-hyde104-98)[[f]](#cite_note-100) The 42-foot-wide (13 m) and 257-foot-long (78 m) structure is described by MDOT as "massive" with an "innovative" construction method: the previous structure was used as a [falsework](/wiki/Falsework "Falsework") for the current bridge before removal.[[95]](#cite_note-mdot-PC-101)

## Business route

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=U.S._Route_2_in_Michigan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Business route")]

| [Business plate.svg](/wiki/File:Business_plate.svg)  [Business US Highway 2 marker](/wiki/File:US_2.svg)  Business US Highway 2 | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Location | [Ironwood](/wiki/Ironwood,_Michigan "Ironwood, Michigan") |
| Length | 1.270 mi[[1]](#cite_note-PRFA-1) (2.044 km) |
| Existed | August 1942[[96]](#cite_note-us2bus-102)–present |

**Business U.S. Highway 2** (**Bus. US 2**) is a 1.270-mile (2.044 km) [business route](/wiki/Business_route "Business route") that runs from the Wisconsin state line at the [Montreal River](/wiki/Montreal_River_(Wisconsin-Michigan) "Montreal River (Wisconsin-Michigan)"). The route extends through downtown Ironwood on Silver and Aurora streets before turning northward along Suffolk Street. Bus. US 2 stays on Suffolk Street for a short while until it turns onto Frederick Street. On Frederick Street, Bus. US 2 bears north through a residential area along Douglas Street. The eastern terminus of the route is at its junction with US 2 at the corner of Cloverland Drive and Douglas Street north of downtown.[[1]](#cite_note-PRFA-1)[[97]](#cite_note-googleIW-103)

The business route was created in August 1942 when former M‑54 in Ironwood was renumbered as a business loop of US 2.[[96]](#cite_note-us2bus-102) It was originally a bi-state business connection before the [Wisconsin Department of Transportation](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Transportation "Wisconsin Department of Transportation") decommissioned Bus. US 2 in [Hurley](/wiki/Hurley,_Wisconsin "Hurley, Wisconsin") westward along [State Trunk Highway 77](/wiki/Highway_77_(Wisconsin) "Highway 77 (Wisconsin)") and northward along [US 51](/wiki/U.S._Route_51_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 51 in Wisconsin") in 2002.[[9]](#cite_note-WisDOT01-02-10)[[10]](#cite_note-WisDOT03-04-11)
